q: Stimulus is a detectable change in the internal or external environment which influence a temporary increase of physiological activity or response in the whole plant or any part of plant.
In the plant, there are two types of stimuli such as internal stimuli and external stimuli.
The responses to external stimuli or environmental changes that are produced by the working of various organs in a systematic and controlled manner are known as coordination.
The movement of plant parts is due to various external and internal stimuli.
External stimuli in plants include water, gravity, light intensity and duration, chemicals, temperature and touch.
Internal stimuli or internal control in plants are chemicals called plant hormones.
Plants possess a nervous system like animals to sense things around them and respond to them.
Coordination and response in plants are undertaken by plant hormones or plant growth regulators or phytohormones.
Plant responses have evolved because they provide the plant with some type of selective advantage, making them better adapted to survive and reproduce.
Examples of plant responses include the tropisms, responses to touch, responses to herbivory and responses to abiotic stress.
Growth in plants is an irreversible increase in size of an individual cell or organ or its parts.
The process of development occurs in an ordered fashion within a single diploid cell called zygote.
Cells produced after mitotic divisions differentiate to give required tissue.
Meristematic cells are isodiametric, thin walled, and consist of large nucleus and dense cytoplasm.
Cells of the elongation phase are found around the meristematic cells and are highly vacuolated.
Cells of the maturation phase undergo structural and functional differentiation and thus, these cells develop into a specialized tissue.
Light is required for tissue differentiation, synthesis of photosynthetic pigments and photosynthesis.
Optimum temperature for plant growth is $20-35^{\circ}C$.
Water maintains the turgidity of plants and is essential for enzymatic activities in protoplasm and cell elongation.
Oxygen is necessary for cellular respiration to produce metabolic energy.
Growth regulators are manufactured by living protoplasm.
Phytohormones are essential organic compounds required in very large quantities.
Phytohormones are generally synthesized at the apices of root, stem and in leaves.
Synthesis of phytohormones is under genetic control and influenced by environmental factors.
Auxin, gibberellin and cytokinin are categorized as growth promoters.
Ethylene and abscisic acid can induce dormancy and abscission as growth inhibitors.
Auxins are weak organic acids produced naturally by plants, chemically known as indole acetic acid (IAA).
Actively growing regions of a plant usually produce the largest amounts of auxins.
Auxin inhibits the elongation of primary roots but can stimulate the initiation of lateral and adventitious roots.
Application of auxin to unpollinated pistil makes them develop seedless fruits or parthenocarpy.
Auxin stimulates the elongation of cells in the shoot and promotes cell enlargement.
In younger parts, auxin delays abscission, while in mature parts, it stimulates abscission.
Auxin initiates and promotes cell division in vascular cambium.
Gibberellins include gibberellic acid and are produced in young organs such as apical leaves, developing buds, and germinating seeds.
Gibberellins transport usually by diffusion and may be transported in the xylem and phloem.
Gibberellins can stimulate stem elongation and promote growth of fruits.
Gibberellins stimulate the synthesis of hydrolytic enzymes like amylases and proteases during seed germination.
Cytokinin is a basic hormone and purine (adenine) derivative that primarily acts on cell division.
Cytokinin occurs naturally in regions with high cell division, such as root apices.
Cytokinins induce flowering in plants and promote lateral bud developments in dicots.
Ethylene is the only gaseous phytohormone and is produced naturally from amino acid methionine.
Maximum synthesis of ethylene occurs during the ripening of fruits.
Ethylene is used in post-harvest technology for commercial ripening of fruits because it increases the rate of respiration.
Ethylene causes drooping of leaves and flowers.
Abscisic acid, also known as abscisin or dormin, is an example in the group of hormones that promote dormancy in plants.
Abscisic acid is known as stress hormone and is produced naturally when a plant is exposed to stress like lack of water.
Abscisic acid includes synthesis of carotenoids and acts as antagonist of all growth promoters.
Seed dormancy is the internal inhibition of germination of a normal or viable seed even when placed under most favourable conditions.
Dormancy of seeds can be broken by scarification, stratification, or hormonal treatment.
Epigeal germination occurs when the cotyledons are first pushed above the ground by the rapid elongation of hypocotyl.
Hypogeal germination is where the epicotyl elongates faster than hypocotyl at the time of seed germination.
Vivipary is the germination of seeds inside the fruit while it is still attached to the parent tree.
Short day plants are long night plants that require shorter photoperiod than their critical photoperiod to initiate flowering.
Day neutral plants are indeterminate plants whose floral initiation is independent of photoperiodism.
Photomorphogenesis is induced by red, far-red and blue light and involves inhibition of stem elongation.
Phytochrome is a protein containing covalently attached chromophore and is a blue-green pigment.
Coordination in animals is the process involved in the detection of stimuli by receptors and subsequent actions of the effectors.
The endocrine system is involved in the transmission of nerve impulses.
The main purpose for living organisms respond to certain stimuli is to ensure the continuity of life or survival of the organisms.
Homeostasis is the regulation of the internal environment of living organisms.
The central nervous system is composed of the brain and the spinal cord which are located at the central axis of the body.
The forebrain comprises the cerebrum, thalamus and hypothalamus.
The hindbrain consists of the cerebellum, pons and medulla oblongata.
The brain is fragile and has a gelatin-like consistency, protected by the skull and meninges.
Cerebrum accounts for more than four fifths of the total weight of the brain and acts as the centres for intellect, learning and memory.
Thalamus acts as "the great relay station" of the brain and provides connections between various parts of the brain.
Hypothalamus contains neurones that control blood pressure, heart rate, body temperature, and basic drives such as thirst and hunger.
Midbrain is involved in processing information from sensory neurones in the eyes, ears, and nose.
Cerebellum receives information from specialized sensors called proprioceptors located within skeletal muscles and joints.
Medulla oblongata coordinates many reflexes and automatic bodily functions that maintain homeostasis.
A cross section of the spinal cord reveals both white matter and grey matter, where the butterfly-shaped core is made up of grey matter.
The somatic nervous system is under voluntary control and services the head, trunk, and limbs.
The vagus nerve is a cranial nerve that has branches to the throat and larynx, but also connects to many internal organs.
The autonomic nervous system is under automatic, or involuntary control, and maintains homeostasis by adjusting to variations in environment.
Reflex arcs are simple connections of only three neurones to transmit messages.
The sympathetic neurones release a neurotransmitter called norepinephrine, which has an excitatory effect on its target muscles.
The parasympathetic nervous system uses the neurotransmitter acetylcholine to control organ responses.
Glial cells nourish the neurones, remove their wastes, and defend against infection.
Dendrites are short, branching terminals of dendron that receive nerve impulses from other neurones or sensory receptors.
The axon conducts impulses away from the cell body and can range in length from $1 mm$ to $1 m$.
Functions of the myelin sheath include protecting the nerve fibre, supplying nutrients to axon, and acting as an insulator to the nerve impulses.
Multipolar neurones have several dendrites and a single axon and are found in the brain and spinal cord.
Bipolar neurones have a single main dendrite and a single axon and are found in the inner ear, retina, and olfactory area.
A neuromuscular junction is a synapse between a motor neurone and a muscle cell.
Synaptic vesicles release their contents into the synaptic cleft, which is only about $0.02 \mu$ wide, by exocytosis.
Upon reaching the postsynaptic membrane, neurotransmitters bind to specific receptor proteins to depolarize the membrane.
If the neurotransmitter is inhibitory, the receptor will trigger potassium channels to open, allowing potassium ions to flow out.
Acetylcholine excites the muscle cell membrane, causing depolarization and contraction of the muscle fibre.
Inadequate production of dopamine is linked to Alzheimer's disease.
Serotonin regulates temperature and sensory perception and is involved in mood control.
Endorphins act as natural painkillers in the brain and affect emotional areas of the brain.
Sense organs convert the stimulus into electrical signals or action potential which are then sent to the specific region of the brain.
Sclera is the outermost coat of the eyeball, which is tough, fibrous and opaque, and maintains the shape of the eyeball.
Cornea is a transparent layer continuous with the sclera that refracts the light rays that enter the eye.
Conjunctiva is a transparent membrane that covers the exposed part of the eyeball and protects the cornea.
Choroid contains a black pigment that absorbs light and prevents internal reflection of light.
The ciliary body is a thickened region at the front end of the choroid that changes the thickness of the lens during accommodation.
The iris contains circular muscles and radial muscles that change the size of the pupil through antagonistic action.
Retina consists of two types of photoreceptor cells: cone cells and rod cells.
Rod cells are sensitive under low light and are responsible for vision at night.
Cone cells are responsible for colour vision under conditions of high light intensity.
The Fovea (Yellow spot) is situated in line with the optical axis of the lens and provides the clearest vision.
The blind spot is the area where neurones leave the retina and form the optic nerve, and it has no photoreceptors.
Aqueous humour maintains the internal pressure of the eye and provides nutrients for the lens and cornea.
Vitreous humour is a jelly-like substance that prevents collapse of the eye and refracts light rays.
Stereoscopic vision allows a person to judge the distance and depth of objects and to estimate the speed of moving objects more accurately.
Endocrine glands secrete chemical messengers called hormones directly into the bloodstream.
The original Greek meaning of the word hormone is to "excite" or "set in motion."
Hormones typically have faster and shorter acting effects compared to the nervous system.
Steroid hormones, such as testosterone, estrogen, and cortisol, are lipid-based.
Water-soluble hormones include epinephrine, human growth hormone (hGH), thyroxine ($T_4$), and insulin.
Hypothalamus regulates anterior pituitary hormones through hypothalamic releasing and inhibiting hormones.
Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) is secreted by the anterior pituitary to stimulate the thyroid gland to produce thyroxine.
Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) is secreted by the posterior pituitary and promotes the retention of water by the kidneys.
Calcitonin targets bones and kidneys to lower blood calcium levels by inhibiting release and reabsorption of calcium.
Parathyroid hormone (PTH) raises blood calcium levels by stimulating bone cells to release calcium and the intestine to absorb calcium.
The adrenal medulla secretes epinephrine and norepinephrine which are fight-or-flight hormones.
Glucagon raises blood glucose levels by converting glycogen in the liver to glucose.
Progesterone promotes growth of the uterine lining and prevents uterine muscle contractions.
Both the nervous system and endocrine system coordinate the body's activities and need a stimulus to produce a response.
The speed of transmission in the endocrine system is determined by the speed of blood flows through the blood vessels.
